title: The evolving liquidity landscape − speech by Victoria Saporta
summary: Victoria Saporta, Executive Director for Markets at the Bank of England, delivered a speech at the ECB Money Market Conference examining how central bank balance sheet normalisation, higher global government bond issuance, and expanding NBFI presence have coalesced to create a new liquidity environment with increased demand for government bond repo funding. The speech outlines the Bank of England's demand-driven repo-led operating framework, where reserve supply is determined by counterparties' demand rather than explicit targeting.
key_points: Central bank balance sheet normalisation is well underway across major economies, with the BoE, Fed, and ECB unwinding QE at similar paces since 2022
Sterling reserves have decreased by approximately £322bn through QT and TFSME repayments since normalisation began
The Bank's Short-Term Repo (STR) and Indexed Long-Term Repo (ILTR) facilities currently supply £152bn of reserves, up from approximately £12bn in January 2024

The Preferred Minimum Range of Reserves (PMRR) is estimated at £375-540bn based on regular counterpart surveys; reaching the upper range is projected towards end of next year under current facility growth assumptions
The minimum spread for Level A collateral in the ILTR is being increased from 0 to 3 basis points to balance incentives between STR and ILTR facilities
Recent overnight General Collateral gilt repo rates spiked to over 30 basis points above Bank Rate due to TFSME repayments coinciding with October month-end and spillovers from tight US repo markets
why_it_matters: This speech provides critical insight into the structural shifts reshaping money markets as central bank balance sheets normalise. Market participants need to understand that the era of abundant reserves is giving way to an environment where temporary funding spikes are possible due to structural frictions in the repo market. The BoE's elastic reserve supply framework via repo facilities is essential for anchoring short-term rates to Bank Rate, but market participants should expect periodic 'bumps along the road' as the system transitions toward equilibrium reserve levels.
macro_context: The evolving liquidity landscape has macro implications as repo market stress can transmit to broader funding conditions. Higher money market rates relative to policy rates may eventually feed through to broader credit conditions. The BoE acknowledges this interaction between monetary control and financial stability objectives.
market_structure_context: The speech is primarily about market structure. Key structural elements include: (1) transition from scarce-reserve to elastic-reserve operating framework; (2) STR facility allows unlimited reserves against Level A collateral (gilts) at Bank Rate; (3) ILTR supplies reserves weekly for six months against broad collateral; (4) system is 'demand-driven repo-led' where reserve size is determined by counterpart demand; (5) NBFIs are expanding their footprint in government bond markets, increasing repo market complexity; (6) structural frictions create potential for temporary repo rate spikes.
important_numbers: £322bn decrease in sterling reserves from QT and TFSME repayments
£152bn current reserves supplied by STR and ILTR facilities
£12bn reserves supplied by facilities in January 2024
£375-540bn estimated Preferred Minimum Range of Reserves (PMRR)
30 basis points above Bank Rate for recent overnight GC gilt repo rates
3 basis points new minimum spread for Level A collateral in ILTR (increased from 0)
notes: Source is a high-quality speech by a senior BoE official at the ECB Money Market Conference. Published November 7, 2025, approximately 5 months prior to TODAY (2026-04-12). Not stale. Source is directly relevant to market structure, covering central bank liquidity operations, repo market functioning, and NBFI dynamics. The speech discusses structural changes to the UK monetary policy operating framework and their implications for money market functioning. Confidence slightly reduced from 10 due to truncation of source body, though core analytical content and key quantitative details are captured.
